mnemonics.title: QUALITY - Key Concepts
mnemonics.content:
Q - Quality is fitness for use
U - User satisfaction is the ultimate goal
A - Assurance prevents, Control detects
L - Lifecycle costs: 50-80% is maintenance
I - Iceberg effect amplifies defects
T - Testing alone cannot ensure quality
Y - Yield to customer needs first

mnemonics.title: COQ Components
mnemonics.content:
P - Prevention costs (training, planning)
A - Appraisal costs (testing, reviews)
I - Internal failure costs (rework)
L - External failure costs (patches, support)
mnemonics.usage: Remember Cost of Quality components in order of preference: Prevention > Appraisal > Failure

acronyms.usage: Process-focused activities that prevent defects from occurring in the first place
Product-focused activities that detect and correct defects in deliverables
Total cost incurred in achieving product quality - prevention, appraisal, and failure costs
Organizational structure, policies, and procedures for quality management
Organization-wide approach with continuous improvement and customer focus
Framework for improving organizational processes and maturity levels

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pyramid with Prevention at the top (smallest but most important), Appraisal in the middle, and Failure costs at the bottom (largest area = highest costs). Prioritize prevention to minimize the base!
Think of an iceberg - only 10% visible above water (defects found in testing), but 90% hidden below (requirement and design defects that propagate). Catch them early before they multiply!
Balance scale: High Quality on one side increases Productivity on the other because less time spent on rework, bug-fixing, and customer support.
QA = Umbrella (preventive, covers entire process), QC = Magnifying glass (inspecting specific products). Both needed for complete quality management.

analogies.analogy: Like building a house - quality planning is the blueprint, quality assurance is using proper materials and techniques during construction, quality control is the final inspection before handover. Skipping any step leads to expensive repairs later!

A luxury sports car meets all specifications (speed, design, features) but if used for off-roading in mountains, it fails 'fitness for use'. Software must match actual user needs, not just stated requirements.

analogies.analody: Prevention is like wearing a helmet while cycling (avoid injury), Detection is like checking for injuries after an accident. Prevention is always better and cheaper!

analogies.analogy: Similar to learning to drive: Initial (chaotic driving), Managed (following rules), Defined (consistent driving), Quantitatively Managed (optimizing fuel/speed), Optimizing (continuous improvement). CMMI applies the same levels to software processes.
